papers.title: Beyond Accuracy: Unveiling Inefficiency Patterns in Tool-Integrated Reasoning

details.title: Beyond Accuracy: Unveiling Inefficiency Patterns in Tool-Integrated Reasoning
details.abstract: In real-world Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) scenarios, a major source of inefficiency is that the toolcalls create pauses between LLM requests and cause KV-cache eviction. Also, the long, unfiltered response returned by external tools inflates the KV-cache, so each decode step spends more time loading the growing cache and thus becomes steadily slower as context length increases. However, existing efficiency metrics like token counts and toolcall counts fail to capture this real computational cost. To address this, we introduce PTE (Prefill Token Equivalents), a hardware-aware TIR-efficiency metric that unifies internal reasoning and external tool-use costs while explicitly accounting for non-reusable KV-Cache and long-tool-response scenarios, thus better reflects real-world scenarios. We conduct extensive experiments across five TIR benchmarks, quantify their PTE costs, and identify four inefficiency patterns that appear in TIR. In a simulated high-concurrency industrial setting, PTE explains wall-clock latency significantly better than token-count metric. We also discover that trajectories with higher PTE costs tend to have lower reasoning correctness, indicating that simply using more tools does not improve the quality of the answer. PTE offers a new perspective on the efficiency of Tool-Integrated Reasoning. The code is available.

papers.title: FactReview: Evidence-Grounded Reviews with Literature Positioning and Execution-Based Claim Verification

details.title: FactReview: Evidence-Grounded Reviews with Literature Positioning and Execution-Based Claim Verification
details.abstract: Peer review in ML is under real pressure — submission volumes keep growing, but reviewer bandwidth doesn't scale with it. This paper tackles a critical piece of the problem: grounding automated reviews in actual evidence rather than just reading the manuscript narrative. FactReview decomposes reviewing into claim extraction, literature positioning, and execution-based verification, which mirrors what a thorough human reviewer would do given enough time. The CompGCN case study makes this concrete — the system reproduces results that largely hold for link prediction and node classification, but correctly narrows the broad performance claim when graph classification tells a different story. That kind of nuanced, evidence-backed assessment is exactly what's missing from current LLM-based reviewing approaches. The backend sensitivity analysis is a valuable addition: showing that verification quality varies substantially across models (from 41.7% to 83.3%) reinforces that this is not just a scripting problem but requires genuine reasoning about the connection between code outputs and paper claims. The failure taxonomy further clarifies where the real bottlenecks lie. The framing here is worth highlighting — positioning AI as an evidence-gathering tool for reviewers rather than a replacement for human judgment is both pragmatic and well-supported by the results.
